text: Robotic ventral hernia. One of the greatest advancements with the use of robotics in general surgery has been the development of ventral hernia repair and abdominal wall reconstruction. Using the robotic system, we now have the capability to perform extensive adhesiolysis using flexible scissors and dissection hooks in a way not previously possible.

The surgeon uses wristed robotic instruments to close primary hernia defects, enhancing the functional architecture of the abdominal wall. The mesh is sutured to the abdominal fascia with precise control.

The result is a hernia patient with a much more functional repair, a patient with lower rates of hernia recurrence, and significantly less wound complications. In addition, there's markedly less pain postoperatively.
The surgical field reveals two hernia defects, with the larger defect located near the umbilicus, characteristic of an incisional hernia developed after a previous operation.

The surgeon clears the peritoneum and preperitoneal fat to expose the posterior rectus fascia, preparing a clean surface for mesh placement against the abdominal wall. After clearing sufficient surface area, the focus shifts to closing the hernia defect.

The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to assist in the process, while a zero-sized V-lock barb suture is passed through one of the ports to begin closing the hernia defect. The suture's one-way barbs help retain tension with each pass.
The surgeon makes relaxing incisions laterally in the posterior rectus fascia to reduce tension on the closure, ensuring these incisions will be covered with mesh later in the procedure.
The surgeon closes the abdominal wall defect, enhancing the integrity of the repair by reinforcing it with mesh, aiming for a strong repair and reduced recurrence rate.

After completely closing the defect, the surgeon introduces the Ethicon PhysioMesh through an eight-millimeter port. A needle is attached to the center of the mesh, which is then passed through the fascial closure and out through the abdominal wall, securing it in place for suturing.

The surgeon uses endo-wrist instrumentation to suture the mesh to the abdominal wall, mimicking open surgery techniques. The sutures are placed circumferentially, ensuring secure fascial purchase with each bite.

This also makes robotic hernia repair less expensive on a case by case basis when compared to laparoscopic repairs due to the need for less disposable instrumentation. It is more efficient, more effective, and absolutely less painful for the patient.

In our own practice series, Out of two hundred and seventy repairs, we have had three recurrences, which is a one point one recurrence rate over the past three years, and a three point three rate of seroma formation, none of which have been clinically significant. In addition, there have been no serious wound infections.

Thus far in our experience, a very effective and safe approach to hernia repair. In addition, Since it is almost identical to suturing during an open procedure, the learning curve for new robotic surgeons is virtually non-existent.

Operative times have been comparable with laparoscopic operative times as well as open procedures. One of the myths perpetuated about robotic surgery is that longer operating times and longer setup times are a necessary evil.

However, our center has debunked both of those myths and shown that a team approach with a dedication to efficiency can produce equal times in the operating room as well as turnover time between cases.

The surgeon completes the hernia repair with a robust primary closure of the defect. A symmetric, tension-free mesh is placed without overlap, and a circumferential suture line is secured to the fascia, resulting in a typical outcome.
